description: Essential phrases for shopping in Finnish stores and markets

items.notes: 'Paljonko' means 'how much'. 'Maksaa' is the verb 'to cost/to pay'. This is the most essential shopping phrase in Finnish.

'Haluaisin' is the conditional form of 'haluta' (to want), making the request polite. 'Ostaa' means 'to buy'. Follow with the item in partitive case for uncountable items or accusative for specific items.
'Teillä' is the formal/plural 'you' in the adessive case. Finnish expresses 'having' with the structure 'onko X:lla/llä' (is there on X). Use 'teillä' when addressing shop staff politely.
'Voinko' is 'can I' from 'voida'. 'Kokeilla' means 'to try/try on'. 'Tätä' is the partitive form of 'tämä' (this). Used when trying on clothes.
'Sovituskoppi' is a compound word from 'sovitus' (fitting) + 'koppi' (booth). Finnish clothing stores always have fitting rooms available.
'Alennuksessa' is the inessive case of 'alennus' (discount/sale). Finnish stores have major sales ('ale') especially in January and July. Look for 'ALE' signs in shop windows.
'Liian' means 'too (much)'. 'Kallis' means 'expensive'. Note that bargaining is not common in Finnish stores, but this phrase is useful for expressing your budget.
'Otan' is the first person of 'ottaa' (to take). 'Tämän' is the genitive/accusative form of 'tämä'. Use this when you've decided to purchase something.
'Voitteko' is the formal/plural 'can you' from 'voida'. 'Auttaa' means 'to help'. 'Minua' is the partitive of 'minä'. Use this polite form when addressing shop staff.
'Kassa' means both 'cash register' and 'checkout'. In Finnish supermarkets, self-checkout ('itsepalvelukassa') is increasingly common.

'Maksan' is the first person of 'maksaa' (to pay). 'Kortilla' is the adessive case of 'kortti' (card), expressing the instrument of payment. Finland is largely cashless; card payment is accepted almost everywhere.
'Käteisellä' is the adessive case of 'käteinen' (cash). While Finland is very card-oriented, cash is still accepted in most places. Some small shops may only accept cards.
'Kuittia' is the partitive of 'kuitti' (receipt). 'Tarvita' takes the partitive case. Receipts are important for returns and warranty claims in Finland.

items.english: I would like to return this.
items.notes: 'Palauttaa' means 'to return (an item)'. Most Finnish stores have a 14-day return policy. Keep your receipt ('kuitti') for returns.
'Mikä' means 'what/which'. 'Koko' means 'size'. Finland uses European clothing sizes. Shoe sizes follow the EU system (e.g., 38, 42).

items.english: Do you have a bigger size?
items.notes: 'Isompaa' is the partitive of the comparative 'isompi' (bigger, from 'iso'). 'Kokoa' is the partitive of 'koko' (size). The partitive is used because we're asking about an unspecified larger size.

items.english: Do you have a smaller size?
items.notes: 'Pienempää' is the partitive of the comparative 'pienempi' (smaller, from 'pieni'). Same grammatical structure as asking for a bigger size.
'Milloin' means 'when'. 'Kauppa' means 'store/shop'. 'Sulkeutuu' is the reflexive form of 'sulkea' (to close). Most Finnish stores close between 18:00-21:00 on weekdays, shorter hours on weekends.
'Ruokaosasto' is a compound word from 'ruoka' (food) + 'osasto' (section/department). Used in large department stores or hypermarkets like Prisma and K-Citymarket.
'Kuinka paljon' means 'how much'. 'Painaa' means 'to weigh'. Useful at market stalls and delis where items are sold by weight. Finland uses the metric system (kilograms and grams).
'Saanko' means 'may I/can I' from 'saada' (to get/may). 'Maistaa' means 'to taste'. Polite way to ask for a sample at a market or food counter.
'Muovipussia' is the partitive of 'muovipussi' (plastic bag). In Finland, plastic bags cost extra (usually 0.10-0.30 euros). Many Finns bring their own reusable bags ('kangaskassi').
'Tarjouksia' is the partitive plural of 'tarjous' (offer/deal). Finnish supermarkets regularly have weekly offers advertised in flyers and at store entrances.

A shortened way of asking for gift wrapping. More explicitly: 'Haluaisin lahjapaketoinnin' (I'd like gift wrapping). 'Lahja' means 'gift'. Gift wrapping services are available in many Finnish stores, especially before Christmas.
'Riittää' means 'is enough/suffices' from 'riittää' (to suffice). Useful at delis, market stalls, or when a salesperson is adding items. A polite way to indicate you have enough.
